questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: In the context of India,
which one of the following is the correct relationship between Rights and Duties?
questions.questionText: In the context of India, which one of the following is the correct relationship between Rights and Duties?

options.b: Rights are personal and hence independent of society and Duties.
options.c: Rights, not Duties, are important for the advancement of the personality of the citizen.
options.d: Duties, not Rights, are important for the stability of the Stat

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **With reference to the Constitution of India, it**can be said that the Rights are correlative with Duties**. Every right has a corresponding duty. Therefore, there can be no duty unless there is someone to whom it is due. There can be no right without a corresponding duty or a duty without a corresponding right. Likewise, every right is right against some person or persons upon whom a correlative duty is imposed. Every right or duty involves a legal obligation by which two or more people are bound together.
Under Indian Constitution, Part III deals with the Fundamental Rights which are certain basic human rights to its people. Our Constitution as originally enacted did not expressly lay down any Fundamental Duties to be performed by citizens. It was only in 1976 that a specific Chapter IV-A was incorporated in the Constitution by a constitutional amendment and Article 51-A was enacted. The idea behind the incorporation of Fundamental Duties was to emphasize the obligation of the citizen in exchange of Fundamental Rights enjoyed by them.

statementLines: Consider the following categories of person:
I. Economically Weaker Sections of citizens
II. Backward classes of citizens
III. Women The Constitution of India explicitly mentions
how many of the above categories for the purpose of providing reservation under Article 16 (Equality of opportunity in matters of public employment)?
questions.questionText: Consider the following categories of person: I. Economically Weaker Sections of citizens II. Backward classes of citizens III. Women The Constitution of India explicitly mentions how many of the above categories for the purpose of providing reservation under Article 16 (Equality of opportunity in matters of public employment)?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Article 16**forms a part of the broader framework of**Right to Equality (Articles 14-18)**under Part III of the Constitution. It**specifically guarantees equality of opportunity in matters of public employment **and aims to ensure that**no **citizen is discriminated against in government jobs on grounds**such as**religion, race, caste, sex, descent, place **of birth, or residence**. At the same time, Article 16 recognizes the**need for positive discrimination**to uplift historically disadvantaged sections of society. Therefore, it**permits the State to make reservations**in appointments or posts for**Backward Classes**,**Scheduled **Castes**(SCs), and**Scheduled Tribes**(STs) in order to achieve their adequate representation in public services.** Option I is correct: **Economically Weaker Sections (EWS) - Eligible for reservation under Article 16**
The **103rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 2019 inserted Article 16(6)** into the Constitution. It empowers the State to make provisions for reservation in appointments or posts in favour of economically weaker sections of citizens, other than those covered under SCs, STs, and OBCs.
This clause provides up to 10% reservation in public employment based purely on economic criteria. Hence, **EWS are explicitly provided reservation under Article 16(6).**

**Option II is correct: Backward Classes-Eligible for reservation under Article 16 of the constitution of India: Article 16(4) allows**the**State to make provisions for**the**reservation of appointments or posts**in **favor of any backward class of citizens** not adequately represented in State services.
The OBCs fall under this description, as upheld in **Indra Sawhney v. Union of India** (1992), which validated reservation for OBCs in employment.
Hence, **OBCs are entitled to reservation under Article 16(4)**

**Option III is incorrect: Women-Not covered under Article 16** The **Constitution does not provide for reservation for women in public employment under Article 16.** However, **Article 15(3)** empowers the State to make special provisions for women and children in other areas such as education and welfare, but not in employment.
Some States may provide horizontal reservation for women through policy decisions, but these are not constitutional provisions under Article 16. Knowledge Base:** The Supreme Court in NALSA v. Union of India, 2014 recognized Transgenders as a socially and educationally backward class entitled to reservations in public employment under Article 16 of the Constitution of India.

source.sourceText: https://ncert.nic.in/textbook.php?keps2=2-10 Page: 32. Mastering Indian Polity by Forum IAS, 1st Edition, Chapter: 10 - Fundamental Rights, Page: 85-86.

statementLines: With reference to the provisions regarding Preventive Detention under the Indian Constitution, consider the following statements:
I. No person can be detained under any preventive detention law for more than three months unless such detention is approved by an Advisory Board.
II. Only Parliament can make laws with regards to preventive detention in India.
III. The constitutional protection against preventive detention is available to both citizens and non-citizens.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the provisions regarding Preventive Detention under the Indian Constitution, consider the following statements: I. No person can be detained under any preventive detention law for more than three months unless such detention is approved by an Advisory Board. II. Only Parliament can make laws with regards to preventive detention in India. III. The constitutional protection against preventive detention is available to both citizens and non-citizens. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Preventive detention**refers to the**detention of a person to prevent the possible commission of an offence**rather than to punish for an act already committed. The Constitution of India, under**Articles 22(3) to 22(7),**expressly**permits preventive detention**, making India one of the few democratic countries to constitutionally sanction such a measure.**

Statement I is correct: **Under**Article 22(4)**of the Constitution,**no person can be detained under any preventive **detention law for a period exceeding three months unless such detention is approved by an Advisory Board. **The Advisory Board must consist of persons who are, or have been, or are qualified to be appointed as, Judges of a High Court, and it examines whether sufficient cause exists for the continued detention. If the Board reports that there is no sufficient cause, the person must be released immediately.**

Statement II is incorrect:**The**Constitution divides the legislative power on preventive detention between the **Parliament and the State Legislatures.**Parliament alone can make laws on preventive detention for reasons related to defence, foreign affairs, and the security of India, while both Parliament and State Legislatures can legislate on matters concerning a State's security, public order, and the maintenance of essential supplies and services. Hence, the**power is not exclusive to Parliament**, but**shared between the Union and the States.**

Statement III is correct: **The c**onstitutional protection concerning preventive detention, applies to all persons, **whether citizens or non-citizens**. Similarly, the procedural safeguards relating to arrest and detention under Articles 22(1) and 22(2) are also available to both categories of persons. Thus, these provisions ensure that even non-citizens enjoy constitutional protection against arbitrary preventive detention. Knowledge Base:**Safeguards and Rights of the Detenu under Preventive Detention: **Communication of Grounds: **The grounds of detention must be**communicated to the detenu**to inform them of the reasons for their detention. However, the**State may withhold facts**if their disclosure is considered**against **the public interest**. Right to Representation: **The detenu must be**given an opportunity to make a representation**against the detention order.**Major Laws on Preventive Detention:** National Security Act (NSA), 1980 Conservation of Foreign Exchange and Prevention of Smuggling Activities (COFEPOSA) Act, 1974 Prevention of Black Marketing and Maintenance of Supplies of Essential Commodities Act, 1980.

source.sourceText: https://ncert.nic.in/textbook.php?keps2=2-10 Page: 35. Mastering Indian Polity by Forum IAS, 1st Edition, Chapter: 10 - Fundamental Rights, Page: 107-110.

statementLines: With reference to the protection in respect of conviction for criminal offences under the Constitution of India:
I. No person shall be punished for an act that was not an offence at the time of its commission.
II. No person shall be prosecuted and punished for the same offence more than once.
III. No person accused of an offence shall be compelled to be a witness against him/herself.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the protection in respect of conviction for criminal offences under the Constitution of India: I. No person shall be punished for an act that was not an offence at the time of its commission. II. No person shall be prosecuted and punished for the same offence more than once. III. No person accused of an offence shall be compelled to be a witness against him/herself. Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **The Constitution of India ensures Protection in respect for conviction for offences as per Article 20.** Option I is correct: **This is known as the**protection against ex post facto laws under Article 20(1).**It means a person cannot be punished under a law that was enacted after the act was committed, i.e., laws cannot operate retrospectively to the disadvantage of the accused. Exception under Article 20(1): While retrospective criminal laws are barred, civil and tax laws can be applied retrospectively. For instance, in the Vodafone case (2012), India amended the Income Tax Act retrospectively to tax a transaction from 2007. Thus, this safeguard is with respect to criminal laws and not civil laws.** Option II is correct: **This is the**safeguard against double jeopardy under Article 20(2).**It ensures that a person once convicted or acquitted of an offence cannot be prosecuted and punished for the same offence more than once. Though this protection is limited to proceedings before a court of law or judicial tribunal and does not apply to administrative or departmental proceedings.** Option III is correct: **This protection is guaranteed under**Article 20(3). **It upholds the principle of "nemo tenetur se ipsum accusare": no one is bound to accuse themselves. It**prevents self
- **incrimination**, meaning the accused cannot be forced to give evidence that may be used against them.

source.sourceText: https://ncert.nic.in/textbook.php?keps2=2-10 Page: 36. Mastering Indian Polity by Forum IAS, 1st Edition, Chapter: 10 - Fundamental Rights, Page: 95-96.

statementLines: Which one of the following Articles was defended by Dr. BR Ambedkar on the plea that it would be used as 'a matter of last resort'?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following Articles was defended by Dr. BR Ambedkar on the plea that it would be used as 'a matter of last resort'?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **During the debates of the Constituent Assembly, Dr. B.R. Ambedkar, the principal architect of the Indian Constitution,**defended Article-356 on the plea that it would be used as "a matter of last resort." **He argued that this provision would only be invoked in exceptional circumstances when all other remedies and alternatives have been exhausted. The intention behind Article-356 was to ensure the maintenance of constitutional order and stability in the country by providing a mechanism to address critical situations in the states.

statementLines: With reference to Fundamental Rights enshrined in the Indian Constitution, consider the following pairs:
Situation - Fundamental Right Violated
I. A 13-year-old child is working in a carpet manufacturing factory. - Right against Exploitation
II. A State government prohibits labourers from other states to work in its own state. - Right to Freedom
III. A group is denied permission to agitate for the protection of Telugu language in Kerala by the Kerala state government. - Cultural and Educational Rights
IV. A female officer in the armed forces is denied promotion on gender basis. - Right to Equality
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.questionText: With reference to Fundamental Rights enshrined in the Indian Constitution, consider the following pairs: Situation - Fundamental Right Violated I. A 13-year-old child is working in a carpet manufacturing factory. - Right against Exploitation II. A State government prohibits labourers from other states to work in its own state. - Right to Freedom III. A group is denied permission to agitate for the protection of Telugu language in Kerala by the Kerala state government. - Cultural and Educational Rights IV. A female officer in the armed forces is denied promotion on gender basis. - Right to Equality How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer**. The Indian Constitution guarantees six**Fundamental Rights (Under Part III)**to all citizens. These rights are enforceable by courts and aim to promote political democracy, protect individual liberties, and prevent authoritarian rule by state.**Pair I is correct**. The Constitution under**Article 24**prohibits the employment of children below the age of 14 in factories, mines, or any other hazardous employment. Carpet manufacturing involves physical strain and exposure to chemicals or dust and hence, it is considered hazardous. Employing a 13-year-old in such a factory is clearly in violation of the**Right against Exploitation**, which protects children from child labour and citizens from trafficking or forced labour.**Pair II is correct**. The Constitution under**Article 19(1)(d)**and**19(1)(e)**gives all citizens the right to move freely throughout India and reside or settle in any part of the territory of India. It also includes the**right to practice any **profession or to carry on any occupation, trade or business**[Article 19(1)(g)]. Stopping labourers from another state violates these freedoms, particularly their**freedom of movement, residence, and employment**.**Pair III is correct**. As per Article 29(1) of the Indian Constitution - 'Any section of the citizens residing in the territory of India or any part thereof having a distinct language, script or culture of its own shall have the right to conserve the same." And as per the Supreme Court judgement the right to conserve the language includes the right to agitate for the protection of the language. Thus,**denying permission to a group of Telugu speaking people to agitate for the protection of their language by **the Kerala state government is a violation of Article 29 (1) which comes under Cultural and Educational Rights. **Pair IV is correct**.**Article 14**guarantees equality before the law and equal protection of laws.**Article 15(1)**prohibits the State from discriminating against any citizen on the basis of sex.**Article 16(1) & (2)**further guarantees equality of opportunity in public employment and prohibits discrimination on the grounds of sex. Denying promotion purely based on gender/sex violates the**Right to Equality**.

source.sourceText: NCERT Class VIII - Social and Political life - III Ch: 1 - The Indian Constitution - Page 14 Mastering Indian Polity by Forum IAS, 1st Edition, Chapter: 10 - Fundamental Rights, Page: 79

statementLines: With reference to issuance of writs by the higher courts in India, consider the following pairs:
Writ - When can it be issued?
I. Mandamus - To punish a person for criminal contempt of the court.
II. Prohibition - When a lower court has considered a case going beyond its jurisdiction.
III. Quo Warranto - When a person is holding a public office but is not entitled to hold that office.
IV. Certiorari - When the court orders release of a person from unlawful detention.
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.questionText: With reference to issuance of writs by the higher courts in India, consider the following pairs: Writ - When can it be issued? I. Mandamus - To punish a person for criminal contempt of the court. II. Prohibition - When a lower court has considered a case going beyond its jurisdiction. III. Quo Warranto - When a person is holding a public office but is not entitled to hold that office. IV. Certiorari - When the court orders release of a person from unlawful detention. How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer**.**Article 32**of the Constitution empowers the Supreme Court to issue writs for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights. A similar power is vested in High Courts under**Article 226**. This right to constitutional remedies is one of the most vital parts of our Constitution. It acts as the guardian of rights and allows citizens to directly approach the courts in case their rights are violated.**Pair I is incorrect**. The writ of**Mandamus**is issued by a court when a public official or public authority fails to perform a**legal or public duty**that is required by law. The word 'Mandamus' means we command. However, to punish a person for**contempt of court**is not the function of this writ.**Contempt of court**is dealt with under**Article 129**in the case of the Supreme Court and**Article 215**in the case of High Courts. It is not part of writ jurisdiction.**Pair II is correct**. The writ of**Prohibition**is issued by a**higher court**such as the Supreme Court or a High Court to a**lower court or tribunal**which is acting**beyond its legal jurisdiction**. The objective of this writ is to stop the lower court from continuing with proceedings that it is not competent to hear. It is a**preventive writ**, issued before the final order is passed.**Pair III is correct**. The writ of**Quo Warranto**is issued to determine the**legal authority of a person holding a public **office**. The literal meaning of the term is "by what authority". This writ is used when a person is occupying a public position without the required qualification or legal entitlement. The court, through this writ,**restrains that person **from continuing in office**.**Pair IV is incorrect**. The writ of**Certiorari**is issued by a higher court to**quash the order or decision of a lower court **or tribunal**when it has acted**without jurisdiction or in violation of the principles of natural justice**. It is a corrective writ and is issued after the lower court has given its decision.**Releasing a person from unlawful detention**can be done through the writ of**Habeas Corpus**.

source.sourceText: https://ncert.nic.in/textbook.php?keps2=2-10 - Right to Constitutional Remedies - Page 41 Mastering Indian Polity by Forum IAS, 1st Edition, Chapter: 10 - Fundamental Rights, Page: 120-121

statementLines: Consider the following statements regarding the National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) of India:
I. It can undertake suo-motu investigations into cases of human rights violations.
II. The members of NHRC are appointed for a term of five years from the date of entering office, and are eligible for reappointment.
III. In 2025 for the first time, the Global Alliance of National Human Rights Institutions (GANHRI) granted 'A' status of accreditation to NHRC, India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements regarding the National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) of India: I. It can undertake suo-motu investigations into cases of human rights violations. II. The members of NHRC are appointed for a term of five years from the date of entering office, and are eligible for reappointment. III. In 2025 for the first time, the Global Alliance of National Human Rights Institutions (GANHRI) granted 'A' status of accreditation to NHRC, India. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct Answer**. The**National Human Rights Commission (NHRC)**was established in**1993**under the**Protection of Human Rights Act, **1993**. It acts as a**watchdog of human rights**in India and works to protect the rights related to life, liberty, equality, and dignity guaranteed by the Constitution or embodied in international covenants. The Commission is a recommendatory and advisory body and it does**not have judicial powers**like a court.**

Statement I is correct**. Under**Section 12a **of the Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993, the NHRC has the power to**inquire, suo motu**, into any violation of human rights, or negligence in the prevention of such violation by a public servant. This means that the Commission can act on its own initiative, without waiting for a formal complaint. For example, if a serious case of custodial death is reported in the media, the NHRC can immediately initiate an independent inquiry. This power makes the Commission proactive in protecting human rights.**

Statement II is incorrect**. According to section 6(2) of the Protection of Human Rights Act ,1993, as amended in 2019, A person appointed as Chairperson shall hold office for a term of three years from the date on which he enters upon his office or until he attains the age of seventy years, whichever is earlier and shall be eligible for re-appointment.**Also, a person appointed as a Member shall hold office for a term of three years from the date on which he enters **upon his office and shall be eligible for re-appointment: Provided that no Member shall hold office after he has **attained the age of seventy years. **On ceasing to hold office, a Chairperson or a Member shall be ineligible for further employment under the Government of India or under the Government of any State. 

Statement III is incorrect**. The Global Alliance of National Human Rights Institutions (GANHRI) is the international body responsible for accrediting National Human Rights Institutions (NHRIs) based on their compliance with the UN Paris Principles ('Principles Relating to the Status of National Human Rights Institutions').**The NHRC, India first **obtained 'A' status accreditation in 1999**. It retained this status in the subsequent review cycles of 2006 and 2011. During the 2016 review, the accreditation process was deferred to the second session held in November 2017, where the Sub-Committee on Accreditation (SCA) of GANHRI recommended that NHRC India be granted 'A' status once again.**In May, 2024, the accreditation to NHRC, India was put on hold and in May 2025, in its 45th session, the Sub
- **Committee on Accreditation (SCA) of GANHRI recommended the downgrading of NHRC status from 'A' to 'B'. The **recommendation of the SCA was challenged by NHRC. It recently said that the recommendation to downgrade will **be implemented in April 2026. ** 'A' status accreditation provides an NHRI with international recognition and protection, as well as the right to participate in GANHRI's work and decision-making. It also enables active engagement with and the right to vote in the UN Human Rights Council.

statementLines: With reference to the provisions contained in Part IV of the Constitution of India, which of the following statements is/are correct?
I. These provisions are not enforceable by any court in India.
II. They list certain rights that individuals should enjoy, apart from those mentioned in the Fundamental Rights of the Constitution of India.
III. They aim to establish social and economic democracy in the society.
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.questionText: With reference to the provisions contained in Part IV of the Constitution of India, which of the following statements is/are correct? I. These provisions are not enforceable by any court in India. II. They list certain rights that individuals should enjoy, apart from those mentioned in the Fundamental Rights of the Constitution of India. III. They aim to establish social and economic democracy in the society. Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer**. The**Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSPs)**are contained in**Part IV of the Indian Constitution**, covering**Articles 36 to 51**. They represent the**guiding principles**that the State should follow in governance. Although they are**not justiciable**in any court of law, they are**fundamental in the governance of the country**, and it shall be the duty of the State to apply them while making laws.**

Statement I is correct**. DPSPs are**non-justiciable**, which means that a citizen cannot move the court to enforce these principles. This is clearly stated in**Article 37**, which says that "the provisions contained in this Part shall not be enforceable by any court." However, the same Article also clarifies that these principles are**nevertheless fundamental **to the governance of the country**, and it shall be the**duty of the State to apply these principles**in making laws. Hence, the judiciary cannot enforce them, but they are not without importance.**

Statement II is correct**. The Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSPs) cover areas such as the right to education, right to adequate means of livelihood, right to equal pay for equal work, and health care and maternity benefits etc. thus helping in realising the idea of a welfare state. Thus**it lists certain rights that individuals should enjoy apart **from the Fundamental Right. **

Statement III is correct**. DPSPs form the**moral compass of the Constitution**. They**aim to foster social and economic **democracy** by directing the State towards equitable distribution of resources , welfare measures for the needy, and a socialist pattern in the society. Many landmark laws like the Right to Education Act or MGNREGA have roots in the vision of the DPSPs.

source.sourceText: https://ncert.nic.in/textbook.php?keps2=2-10 - Directive Principles of State Policy - Page 43; Mastering Indian Polity by Forum IAS, 1st Edition, Chapter: 11 - Directive Principles of State Policy, Page: 129

statementLines: Consider the following statements regarding the Fundamental Duties mentioned in the Indian Constitution:
I. They were added to the Constitution by the 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976.
II. At present, there are a total of ten Fundamental Duties mentioned in the Constitution.
III. The duties mentioned are applicable to both citizens and non-citizens.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements regarding the Fundamental Duties mentioned in the Indian Constitution: I. They were added to the Constitution by the 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976. II. At present, there are a total of ten Fundamental Duties mentioned in the Constitution. III. The duties mentioned are applicable to both citizens and non-citizens. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer**. Fundamental Duties are enshrined in**Part IVA**of the Indian Constitution under**Article 51A**. They were introduced to remind citizens that while they enjoy Fundamental Rights, they also owe certain duties to the nation. These duties reflect the spirit of**civic responsibility**,**national unity**, and**constitutional morality**.**

Statement I is correct**. The**42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976**, added**Part IVA (Article 51A)**to the Constitution and incorporated**ten Fundamental Duties**. This amendment was based on the recommendations of the**Swaran Singh Committee**and was passed during the Emergency period. It marked the first formal inclusion of citizens' duties in the Constitution.**

Statement II is incorrect**. Originally, ten Fundamental Duties were listed when they were introduced in 1976. However, the**86th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2002**inserted an**eleventh duty**under**Article 51A(k)**, which makes it a duty of every parent or guardian to provide opportunities for education to their child between**6 and 14 **years of age**. Therefore, at present, there are**11 Fundamental Duties**, not ten.**

Statement III is incorrect**.** Fundamental Duties are applicable only to citizens of India.** The opening words of Article 51A clearly say, "It shall be the duty of every citizen of India..." Hence, non-citizens or foreigners residing in India are not bound by these duties.

source.sourceText: https://ncert.nic.in/textbook.php?keps2=2-10 - Fundamental Duties of citizens - Page 44; Mastering Indian Polity by Forum IAS, 1st Edition, Chapter: 12 - Fundamental Duties, Page: 139-140

statementLines: Who among the following are entitled to get the benefits of equality before the law and the equal protection of the laws as enshrined under Article-14 of the Constitution of India?
1. All Indian citizens living in India.
2. All Indian citizens living abroad.
3. Foreigners living within the territory of India.
4. All citizens born in India.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: Who among the following are entitled to get the benefits of equality before the law and the equal protection of the laws as enshrined under Article-14 of the Constitution of India? 1. All Indian citizens living in India. 2. All Indian citizens living abroad. 3. Foreigners living within the territory of India. 4. All citizens born in India. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Article 14 of the Constitution of India guarantees equality before the law and equal protection of the laws to all persons**within the territory of India. This means that both citizens and foreigners are entitled to the protection of **Article 14 and it does not apply to Indian citizens living abroad and citizens born in India but living abroad. **

statementLines: Which of the following best describes the concept of Freedom in a democratic society? down by law.
questions.questionText: Which of the following best describes the concept of Freedom in a democratic society? down by law.

options.a: The power to act according to one's own will, without any restriction or external control.
options.b: The right to do anything that promotes one's happiness and self-interest, irrespective of societal norms.
options.c: The access of opportunities to express oneself and pursue one's interests, subject to reasonable restrictions laid
options.d: Absolute protection from arbitrary action of the Stat

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.**

Option a is incorrect.**The power to act according to one's own will, without any restriction or external control reflects the**negative aspect of freedom**, where freedom is understood as the total absence of constraints. However, such absolute freedom is impractical and undesirable in a society, as it can lead to chaos and the violation of others' rights. In a democratic setup, freedom must be balanced with responsibility and respect for the rights of others.**

Option b is incorrect.**The right to do anything that promotes one's happiness and self-interest, irrespective of societal norms, assumes that individuals can act purely for their self-interest, disregarding**moral, legal, or social **considerations**. It undermines the very foundation of a democratic and moral society. Freedom does not mean acting selfishly or harming others for personal gain.**

Option c is correct.**The availability of opportunities to express oneself and pursue one's interests, subject to reasonable restrictions laid down by law, best represents the true democratic understanding of freedom. It**balances **individual liberty with social responsibility**. Citizens are free to think, express, and act according to their choices but within limits that prevent harm to others and maintain public order. The Indian Constitution reflects this understanding through**Fundamental Rights**that guarantee freedom (of speech, movement, association, etc.) while permitting**reasonable restrictions**in the interests of morality, security, and public welfare etc.**

Option d is incorrect.**Absolute protection or freedom from oppression or misuse of power by the government**is a vital component of liberty. However, it captures only one dimension, that is,**political freedom**. True freedom also requires the presence of**social, economic, and personal opportunities** that empower individuals to act autonomously.

source.sourceText: Pg: 19 https://ncert.nic.in/textbook.php?keps1=2-8

statementLines: Consider the following statements: In order to address the inequality in society-
I. Marxists advocate equality of opportunity for all, irrespective of class or status of person, while the liberals advocate equitable share of resources among all persons in the society.
II. Marxists oppose the concentration of wealth in a few hands, while liberals support a fair and competitive system as the most prudent way to allocate resources.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: In order to address the inequality in society- I. Marxists advocate equality of opportunity for all, irrespective of class or status of person, while the liberals advocate equitable share of resources among all persons in the society. II. Marxists oppose the concentration of wealth in a few hands, while liberals support a fair and competitive system as the most prudent way to allocate resources. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.**Marxism**, founded on the ideas of Karl Marx, views private ownership of economic resources such as land, industries, and capital as the root cause of inequality in the society.**Liberalism**, on the other hand, emphasizes individual freedom, competition, and equal opportunity.**

Statement I is incorrect.**Marxists feel that to tackle inequality in society we need to go beyond providing equal opportunities and try to ensure public control over essential resources and forms of property. They aim at a classless society with social or economic equality or equal/collective control over means of production. They advocate equality of outcome or equitable distribution of resources, not merely equality of opportunity. Liberals on the other hand advocate equality of opportunity and not equitable sharing of resources within the members of society.**

Statement II is correct: **According to Marxists**the root cause of entrenched inequality in the society was private **ownership of important economic resources s**uch as oil, or land, or forests, as well as other forms of property. Such private ownership did not only make the class of owners wealthy, it also gave them political power that enabled them to influence state policies and laws and this could prove a threat to democratic government.**Hence in order to **address inequality in society, Marxists advocate for a classless society and oppose concentration of wealth in the **hands of few. **Liberals uphold the principle of competition**as the most efficient and fair way of distributing resources and rewards in society. They believe that while states may have to intervene to try and ensure a minimum standard of living and equal opportunities for all, this cannot by itself bring equality and justice to society.** Competition between people in **free and fair conditions is the most just and efficient way of distributing rewards in a society. **For them,**as long as **competition is open and free, inequalities are unlikely to become entrenched **and people will get due reward for their talents and efforts.

statementLines: With reference to the Indian Constitution, consider the following statements:
I. It prohibits the State from discriminating against any citizen on grounds only of religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth or any of them.
II. It abolishes the practice of untouchability in all forms.
III. The State can make any special provision, by law, for the advancement of any socially and educationally backward classes of citizens related to their admission to educational institutions.
How many of the above provisions prove that the Indian Constitution promotes substantive equality (goes beyond equal treatment) rather than merely formal equality (treating everyone equally)?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Indian Constitution, consider the following statements: I. It prohibits the State from discriminating against any citizen on grounds only of religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth or any of them. II. It abolishes the practice of untouchability in all forms. III. The State can make any special provision, by law, for the advancement of any socially and educationally backward classes of citizens related to their admission to educational institutions. How many of the above provisions prove that the Indian Constitution promotes substantive equality (goes beyond equal treatment) rather than merely formal equality (treating everyone equally)?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.**Equality is a core value enshrined in the**Indian Constitution**. However, there are two levels at which equality operates: formal** equality**and**substantive equality**.**Formal equality**means that the law treats everyone alike.**Substantive equality**, on the other hand, goes beyond identical treatment. It acknowledges that some groups start from a position of deep social and economic disadvantage, and therefore require positive measures or affirmative action to ensure they can truly enjoy equal opportunities.**

Statement I is incorrect.**Article 15(1)**, ensures that the State does not discriminate against citizens on specific grounds. It represents a** step toward establishing formal equality**, as it removes legal and institutional barriers to equal treatment. However, by itself, it does not remove deep-rooted inequalities that exist in society due to centuries of social oppression or economic disadvantage.**

Statement II is incorrect. Article 17**abolishes untouchability and makes its practice an offence. Although it targets a specific and deeply rooted form of social inequality, it does so by**ending social discrimination **that deprives people of equal status and access. It represents a step toward establishing formal equality. It does not, however, confer special or affirmative action to uplift the victims of untouchability.**

Statement III is correct.**As per Article 15 (4) of the Indian Constitution the State can make any special provision for the advancement of any socially and educationally backward classes of citizens or for the Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes. And as per Article 15 (5) the**State can make any special provision, by law, for the advancement of any socially and **educationally backward classes of citizens**or for the Scheduled Castes or the Scheduled Tribes in so far as such**special provisions relate to their admission to educational institutions** including private educational institutions, whether aided or unaided by the State, other than the minority educational institutions referred to in clause (1) of article 30.
Thus, Article 15(4), 15(5) promotes substantive equality (goes beyond equal treatment) rather than merely formal equality (treating everyone equally).

statementLines: Which of the following is/are the consequences of proclamation of the Emergency in a State under Article 356 of the Constitution of India?
I. The State Legislative Assembly gets automatically dissolved.
II. The President may by proclamation declare that the powers of the Legislature of the State shall be exercisable by or under the authority of Parliament.
III. The President is empowered to assume or curtail the powers of any of the State authorities, including the High Court.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: Which of the following is/are the consequences of proclamation of the Emergency in a State under Article 356 of the Constitution of India? I. The State Legislative Assembly gets automatically dissolved. II. The President may by proclamation declare that the powers of the Legislature of the State shall be exercisable by or under the authority of Parliament. III. The President is empowered to assume or curtail the powers of any of the State authorities, including the High Court. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Article 356**of the Constitution empowers the President to impose emergency in a State, also known as the 'State Emergency' or President's Rule. It can be imposed under**Article 356**if a State's governance deviates from the constitutional provisions. Additionally,**Article 365 **specifies that, if a State disregards any directive issued by the Central Government, then also the emergency under**Article 356 **can be imposed.**

Statement I is incorrect. **In the landmark S.R. Bommai judgement of 1994, the Supreme Court clarified that**until **parliamentary approval is secured, the President can only suspend the state legislative assembly but cannot **dissolve it. **

Statement II is correct**. As per Article 356(1)(a) of the Indian Constitution** the President may by Proclamation declare **that the powers of the Legislature of the State shall be exercisable by or under the authority of Parliament. **

Statement III is incorrect. **When Emergency under**Article 356 **is imposed on a State, the President can take up the functions of the State Government and powers vested in the Governor or any other executive authority in the State. However, he cannot assume the powers of the High Court. The High Court retains its constitutional powers, and its functions remain unchanged. Article 356** does not authorise the President to assume to himself any of the powers vested in or exercisable by a **High Court,** or to suspend the operation of any provision of the Constitution relating to High Courts. The constitutional position, powers and functions of the concerned State High Court remain the same even during the operation of emergency under Article 356.

statementLines: Consider the following rights:
I. Right of the workers to resort to strike
II. Right to vote
III. Right to have primary education in one's mother tongue.
IV. Right against taxation without authority of law
How many of the above are recognised as the constitutional rights in India?
questions.questionText: Consider the following rights: I. Right of the workers to resort to strike II. Right to vote III. Right to have primary education in one's mother tongue. IV. Right against taxation without authority of law How many of the above are recognised as the constitutional rights in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. ** Option I is incorrect: Article 19(1)(c) of the Indian Constitution grants citizens the fundamental right to form **associations, unions, or cooperative societies. **The Supreme Court held that while workers have the freedom of association, the Right to Strike is neither a fundamental right nor a Constitutional right.**It is a statutory right **primarily regulated by the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947**.** Option II is correct: Right to vote is recognized as the Constitutional rights under Article 326 of Indian **constitution. **It states that the elections to the Lok Sabha and the State Legislative Assemblies are based on adult suffrage**i.e. every citizen of India aged 18 years or above, who is not disqualified on grounds such as non-residence, **unsoundness of mind, crime, or corrupt practices, is entitled to be registered as a voter. ** Option III is correct: Right to have primary education in one's mother tongue is a constitutional right under **Article 350A of the Indian Constitution. **It provides that it is the duty of every State and local authority to make efforts**to provide adequate facilities for primary education in the mother tongue to children belonging to linguistic **minority groups. ** Option IV is correct: **As per**Article 265 of Indian Constitution**,** The right against taxation without authority of law **is one of the constitutional rights that mandates that no tax can be levied or collected by the state except as **explicitly provided for by an Act of the legislature**.

statementLines: With reference to Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR), consider the following statements:
I. It imposes direct legal obligations on member countries to implement its provisions.
II. The exact wording and structure of the Preamble of the Indian Constitution were borrowed from the preamble of UDHR.
III. It advocates abolition of slavery in all its forms.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR), consider the following statements: I. It imposes direct legal obligations on member countries to implement its provisions. II. The exact wording and structure of the Preamble of the Indian Constitution were borrowed from the preamble of UDHR. III. It advocates abolition of slavery in all its forms. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) is a milestone document in the history of human rights. Drafted by representatives with different legal and cultural backgrounds from all regions of the world, the Declaration was proclaimed by the United Nations General Assembly in Paris on 10 December 1948 (General Assembly resolution 217 A) as a common standard of achievements for all peoples and all nations. It sets out, for the first time, fundamental human rights to be universally protected and it has been translated into over 500 languages. The UDHR is widely recognized as having inspired, and paved the way for, the adoption of more than seventy human rights treaties, applied today on a permanent basis at global and regional levels.**

Statement I is incorrect: **The Universal Declaration of Human Rights is an international document that outlines the basic rights and freedoms of all individuals.** However, it is not a legally binding treaty, and therefore does not **impose direct legal obligations on member countries. **

Statement II is incorrect: The Preamble of the Indian Constitution was influenced primarily from the Objective **Resolution of 1946, **moved by Jawaharlal Nehru in the first session of Constituent Assembly** (not taken directly from **the UDHR). **

Statement III is correct: **Article 4 of the UDHR advocates that** no one shall be held in slavery or servitude; slavery **and the slave trade shall be prohibited in all their forms.**

questions.microTopic: Directive Principles of State Policy DPSP
statementLines: With reference to the Indian Polity, consider the following provisions:
I. To safeguard the forests and wild life of the country.
II. To take steps to organise village panchayats and endow them with such powers as may be necessary to enable them to function as units of self-government.
III. To strive towards securing that all the citizens have the right to an adequate means of livelihood.
IV. To encourage settlements of international disputes through the international Judicial mechanisms like the International Criminal Court.
How many of the above are recognized as duties of the State under Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSPs) of the Indian Constitution?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Indian Polity, consider the following provisions: I. To safeguard the forests and wild life of the country. II. To take steps to organise village panchayats and endow them with such powers as may be necessary to enable them to function as units of self-government. III. To strive towards securing that all the citizens have the right to an adequate means of livelihood. IV. To encourage settlements of international disputes through the international Judicial mechanisms like the International Criminal Court. How many of the above are recognized as duties of the State under Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSPs) of the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Directive Principles of State Policy (Article 36-51) provided under Part IV of Indian constitution are the form of instructions/guidelines to the governments at the centre as well as to the states. These principles are non-justiciable in nature and are fundamental in the governance of the country.** Option I is correct:**According to Article 48A of Indian Constitution, inserted by the 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976, the State shall endeavour to protect and improve the environment from pollution and**to safeguard the **forests and wildlife of the country. ** Option II is correct: Article 40 **directs the State to**"take steps to organise village panchayats and endow them with **such powers and authority as may be necessary to enable them to function as units of self-government.** Option III is correct:**Article 39(a) **of the Indian Constitution directs the State to ensure that** all citizens, both men **and women equally, have the right to an adequate means of livelihood. ** Option IV is incorrect: **According to Article 51(d)of the Constitution**, the State shall endeavour to encourage the **settlement of international disputes by arbitration (not by an international judicial mechanism like International **Criminal Court**).

questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Consider the following:
I. To respect National Anthem and National flag
II. To provide early childhood care and education to all the children up to the age of 6 Years.
III. To foster respect for international laws and treaty obligation among the individuals.
IV. To strive towards excellence in all spheres of individual and collective activity.
How many of the above are mentioned in Part IV-A of the Indian constitution?
questions.questionText: Consider the following: I. To respect National Anthem and National flag II. To provide early childhood care and education to all the children up to the age of 6 Years. III. To foster respect for international laws and treaty obligation among the individuals. IV. To strive towards excellence in all spheres of individual and collective activity. How many of the above are mentioned in Part IV-A of the Indian constitution?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Fundamental Duties are the moral responsibilities of citizens to foster patriotism and uphold the unity and integrity of the nation. They were incorporated into the Constitution by the 42nd Amendment Act, 1976, which added Article 51A under a new Chapter IV-A listing ten duties. Later, the 86th Amendment Act, 2002 added one more duty, making the total eleven Fundamental Duties.** Option I is correct:**According to**Article 51A (a) of the Indian Constitution,**it is the**Fundamental Duty**of every citizen to abide by the Constitution and to respect its ideals and institutions, as well as**to show due respect to the**National Flag and the National Anthem.** Option II is incorrect:**Article 45**, under the**Directive Principles of State Policy (Part IV)(not under Part IV A)**, directs the State to**provide early childhood care and education**for all children until they reach the age of**six years**. As per Article 51A(k), inserted by the 86th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2002, it is the Fundamental Duty of every parent or guardian to ensure education for their child or ward between the**ages of six and fourteen years**.** Option III is incorrect:**To foster respect for international law and treaty obligations is a Directive Principle of **State Policy**under Article 51 (c) of Indian Constitution, and**not a Fundamental Duty of citizens. ** Option IV is correct:**According to**Article 51A(j)**of the Indian Constitution, it is the**Fundamental Duty**of every citizen to**strive for excellence in all spheres of individual and collective activity**, so that the nation continually rises to higher levels of effort and achievement.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Consider the following authorities/bodies:
I. Public Sector Undertakings
II. The Board of Control for Cricket in India
III. Power Distribution Companies (DISCOMS)
IV. National Council of Educational Research and Training (NCERT)
How many of the above given authorities/bodies come under the definition of state under Article 12 of the Indian Constitution?
questions.questionText: Consider the following authorities/bodies: I. Public Sector Undertakings II. The Board of Control for Cricket in India III. Power Distribution Companies (DISCOMS) IV. National Council of Educational Research and Training (NCERT) How many of the above given authorities/bodies come under the definition of state under Article 12 of the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **As per Article 12 of the Indian constitution, the definition of the State includes the Government and Parliament of India and the Government and the Legislature of each of the States and all local or other authorities within the territory of India or under the control of the Government of India. The Supreme Court of India has held in a number of cases to decide the "other authorities" included under Article 12 of Indian Constitution.** Option I is correct:**The Supreme Court of India has held in a number of decisions that**Public Corporations and **Undertakings fall within the inclusive definition of 'State' under Article 12 of Indian Constitution. ** Option II is incorrect:**The**Board of Control for Cricket in India (BCCI)**is**not regarded as a "State" under Article 12**of the Constitution because it is an autonomous body not created by any statute, and no part of its share capital is held by the government. The BCCI receives no substantial financial assistance from the government, and its monopoly over cricket administration in India is neither conferred nor protected by the State.** Option III is correct:**The Supreme Court of India in its recent judgment in Southern Power Distribution Power Co. Ltd. of AP (APSPDCL) v. Hinduja National Power Corpn. Ltd. (HNPCL), held that**DISCOMS are instrumentalities of **the State and fall within the purview of Article 12 of the Constitution of India. ** Option IV is incorrect: In Chander Mohan Khanna vs. NCERT (1991), the Supreme Court held the National Council **of Educational Research and Training (NCERT) does not come under the definition of state under Article 12. **The Court relied on the fact that NCERT was an autonomous body whose activities were not wholly related to governmental functions. The governmental control was low and the government did not fund NCERT wholly. Thus, the case did not satisfy the requirements of the state under Article 12 of the Constitution.

statementLines: The Constitution of India guarantees freedom of speech and expression. But the freedom is subject to certain reasonable restrictions imposed by the state. These restrictions may relate to which of the following?
1. Defamation
2. Decency or morality
3. Incitement to an offence
Select the correct answer using the codes given below.
questions.questionText: The Constitution of India guarantees freedom of speech and expression. But the freedom is subject to certain reasonable restrictions imposed by the state. These restrictions may relate to which of the following? 1. Defamation 2. Decency or morality 3. Incitement to an offence Select the correct answer using the codes given below.
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.**Article 19 (1) (a) provides that 'All citizens shall have the right to freedom of speech and expression. The Constitution also provides that nothing in sub-clause (a) of clause (1) Article 19 shall affect the operation of any existing law, or prevent**the State from making any law, in so far as such law imposes reasonable restrictions on the exercise of the **right conferred by the said sub-clause in the interests of the sovereignty and integrity of India, the security of the **State, friendly relations with foreign States, public order, decency or morality, or in relation to contempt of court, **defamation or incitement to an offence.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XVIII: Emergency Provisions
statementLines: If the Financial Emergency under Article 360 is in operation in India, which of the following would not be an implication of it? suspended. Legislature.
questions.questionText: If the Financial Emergency under Article 360 is in operation in India, which of the following would not be an implication of it? suspended. Legislature.

options.a: The executive authority of the union extends to giving financial directives to any State.
options.b: The Constitutional provisions regarding the distribution of revenues between the Centre and the States gets
options.c: Salaries and allowances of the Judges of the Supreme Court and the High Courts may be reduced.
options.d: All Money Bills of a state may be reserved for the consideration of the President after being passed by the State

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Article 360 of the Constitution of India states that If the President is satisfied that a situation has arisen whereby the financial stability or credit of India or of any part of the territory thereof is threatened, he may by a Proclamation make a declaration to that effect.**Statement a is correct. Article 360(3)**states that**, **the**executive authority of the Union shall extend to the giving of **directions to any State to observe such canons of financial propriety as may be specified in the directions**. Thus union can give financial directives to any State**Statement b is incorrect. **The Constitutional provisions regarding the distribution of revenues between the Centre and the States do not get suspended during a Financial Emergency under**Article 360**. Statement c is correct.**When the Financial Emergency is proclaimed, Under** Article 360(4)(b) the President can issue **directives for the reduction of salaries and allowances**of all or any class of persons serving in connection with the affairs of the Union, as well as**judges of the Supreme Court and High Courts.**Statement d is correct.**Under Article**360(4)(ii)**, a State may be directed to reserve all Money Bills, or other Bills to which Article 207 applies, for the consideration of the President** after they are passed by the State Legislature.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XVIII: Emergency Provisions
statementLines: The Shah Commission of Inquiry, set up in 1977 by the Government of India, was primarily appointed to inquire into which of the following?
questions.questionText: The Shah Commission of Inquiry, set up in 1977 by the Government of India, was primarily appointed to inquire into which of the following?

options.a: Excesses and misuse of authority during the Emergency period (1975-77).
options.b: Misuse of President's Rule in various States during the 1970s.
options.c: Allegations of irregularities in defence procurement during the 1971 war.
options.d: Implementation of the recommendations of the Swaran Singh Committe

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **The**Shah Commission of Inquiry was constituted in May 1977 by the Janata Party government**headed by**Prime **Minister Morarji Desai. **It was chaired by**Justice J.C. Shah**, a retired Chief Justice of India. The**Commission was tasked with investigating **the**abuse of authority, violations of civil liberties, arbitrary **arrests, press censorship**, and** misuse of government machinery that occurred during the Emergency**(June 1975-March 1977) declared by**Prime Minister Indira Gandhi**.
The Shah Commission's findings exposed serious administrative and political excesses, leading to public debate on constitutional safeguards and greater emphasis on protecting fundamental rights in subsequent years.

source.sourceText: https://ncert.nic.in/textbook/pdf/leps206.pdf Page: 103. https://www.pib.gov.in/FactsheetDetails.aspx?Id=149224&reg=3&lang=1 Mastering Indian Polity by Forum IAS, 1st Edition, Chapter: 14 - Provisions of Emergency, Page: 188

statementLines: Consider the following statements:
Statement I: The Part III of the Indian Constitution is often referred to as the Magna Carta of India.
Statement II: The Part III of the Indian Constitution guarantees fundamental rights to citizens and imposes limitations on the authority of the State.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: Statement I: The Part III of the Indian Constitution is often referred to as the Magna Carta of India. Statement II: The Part III of the Indian Constitution guarantees fundamental rights to citizens and imposes limitations on the authority of the State. Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is not correct
options.d: Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Part III**of the Indian Constitution, covering**Articles 12 to 35, deals with Fundamental Rights.**These rights ensure the protection of individual liberty, the promotion of equality, and the establishment of rule of law in the country. Inspired by the**American Bill of Rights**and influenced by the**Magna Carta**, these rights are**justiciable,**meaning that any violation can be challenged in a court of law. 

Statement I is correct: Part III**of the Indian Constitution**is often called the "Magna Carta of India"**because it lays down the foundation of individual freedom and civil liberties in the country, much like the**Magna Carta of 1215 did in **England**by**curbing the absolute powers of the monarchy and securing basic rights for citizens**.**

Statement II is correct: Part III guarantees Fundamental Rights**such as equality, freedom, protection against exploitation, freedom of religion, cultural and educational rights, and the right to constitutional remedies.**These **rights impose limitations on the authority of the State**, ensuring that individual liberty and human dignity are protected against arbitrary state actions.**Link between both statements: **Statement II provides the reason and explanation for Statement I.**It is precisely because Part III guarantees Fundamental Rights and limits State power to uphold liberty that it is regarded as the Magna Carta of India. Hence,**Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I**.

questions.microTopic: Directive Principles of State Policy DPSP
statementLines: The provision, "the State shall take steps to separate the Judiciary from the Executive in the public services of the State" is incorporated in which part of the Constitution of India?
questions.questionText: The provision, "the State shall take steps to separate the Judiciary from the Executive in the public services of the State" is incorporated in which part of the Constitution of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **The Constitution of India includes provisions in Part IV (Article 36-51) known as the Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP). One such principle is highlighted in**Article 50, which emphasizes the importance of separating the judiciary from **the executive in the public services of the State.** This provision reflects the commitment to maintaining the independence and impartiality of the judiciary, ensuring a fair and just system of governance.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Consider the following scenarios:
I. A law passed by the legislature, which has a provision that a juvenile will receive a lighter punishment compared to an adult for committing the same heinous crime.
II. A law passed by the legislature, which has a provision that a person under sentence of imprisonment for life, commits murder, shall be punished with death.

III. A Government department sent an order where the workmen employed by the department on famine relief work will be excluded from the Minimum Wages act. In which of the above scenarios is there no violation of the principle of 'equal protection of laws' as provided under Article 14 of the Constitution of India?
questions.questionText: Consider the following scenarios: I. A law passed by the legislature, which has a provision that a juvenile will receive a lighter punishment compared to an adult for committing the same heinous crime. II. A law passed by the legislature, which has a provision that a person under sentence of imprisonment for life, commits murder, shall be punished with death. III. A Government department sent an order where the workmen employed by the department on famine relief work will be excluded from the Minimum Wages act. In which of the above scenarios is there no violation of the principle of 'equal protection of laws' as provided under Article 14 of the Constitution of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Article 14 of the Constitution of India says that the**State shall not deny to any person equality before the law or the **equal protection of the laws **within the territory of India. This article has two components: Equality before the Law, and Equal protection of the Laws.**'Equality before the law'**ensures that all individuals are subjected equally to the ordinary law of the land.**It is a **negative concept as the State is restricted from making any discrimination between two citizens**. It**dictates that **there should be no special privileges**granted to any person and all individuals are subject to the same laws administered by ordinary courts. The concept of** 'Equal Protection of Laws' **implies that individuals should be treated equally in similar circumstances and unequally under unequal circumstances. It is a positive concept. It**allows unequal treatment to persons placed **differently**.**

Statement I is correct: **This scenario is an** example of equal protection of laws**because juveniles and adults are not similarly situated in terms of mental maturity and culpability. Therefore, differential treatment is allowed and justified. For example, under the**Juvenile Justice Act,2015**offenders who have not completed 18 years of age(Juvenile) receive reformative rather than retributive punishment. The classification is based on age and the objective is rehabilitation, which makes the differential treatment legally valid. Therefore**there is no violation of the principle of 'equal **protection of laws' as provided under Article 14 of the Constitution of India in this scenari**o.**

Statement II is incorrect: **As per various Supreme Court judgements, with respect to equal protection of law the reasonable classification must not be arbitrary, artificial or evasive but must be based on some real and substantial distinction bearing a just and reasonable relation to the object sought to be achieved by the legislation. In the above scenario the classification is not justified, as** classification between persons who commit murders **whilst under the sentence of the imprisonment and those who commit murders whilst they were not under the **sentence of life imprisonment for the purpose of making the sentence of death mandatory in the case of the **former class and optional in the latter class was not based on any rational principle. **Eg. The Supreme Court in Mithu v. State of Punjab, 1995 struck down Section 303 of Indian Penal code (Punishment for murder by life-convict) on the ground of unconstitutional under Article 14.**

Statement III is incorrect: In this scenario also the classification is not justified, as rights of all the workers will be **the same whether they are drawn from an area affected by drought and scarcity conditions or come from **elsewhere. This is not a reasonable classification and goes against the doctrine of classification. ** Eg: The Supreme Court in Sanjit Roy vs State Of. Rajasthan, 1983 ruled that Exclusion from the Minimum Wages Act, of the workmen employed by government on famine relief work, and payment to them of wages lower than the minimum wages, violates article 14.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: In the context of Indian Polity,
which one of the following is the most appropriate description of 'rule of law'?
questions.questionText: In the context of Indian Polity, which one of the following is the most appropriate description of 'rule of law'?

options.a: The Government exercises its authority only through the laws enacted by it.
options.b: Individuals have the liberty of action but within the confines of the law.
options.c: The law is supreme and applies equally to all the individuals.
options.d: A law must be followed even if it is against the basic ethos of society.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **

Option a is incorrect. **This**statement describes only the legality of government action and not the broader concept **of the Rule of Law.**The Rule of Law requires more than merely acting through laws:**it demands the absence of **arbitrariness, equality before the law, and the supremacy of law.**Even authoritarian regimes act strictly through laws that they enact, yet they violate the Rule of Law by enforcing discriminatory, unjust, or arbitrary laws. Therefore, the statement is too narrow and does not convey the essential meaning of the Rule of Law.**

Option b is incorrect. **This statement**describes liberty and legal restrictions, not the Rule of Law**. Every legal system restricts liberty within the confines of the law, but this does not explain the essential elements of the Rule of Law such as the supremacy of law and absence of arbitrariness. It**presents only a citizen-centric view**and ignores the fact that the State itself must be bound by law, which is the core of the Rule of Law.**

Option c is correct. Rule of law means that all laws apply equally to all citizens of the country**. It implies that no one is above the law, whether the government, a wealthy person, or even the President of the country. India has adopted the concept of the Rule of Law from British Common Laws. According to A.V. Dicey, there are three elements of the Rule of Law, which are: 1. Supremacy of Law: It means that the**law is supreme, and no one, including the government, is above the law. **2. Equality before the Law: It ensures that all individuals, regardless of their social or economic status, are**subject to **the same laws**and are equally accountable before the ordinary courts of the land. 3. Primacy of Individual rights: This element suggests that the rights of the individual are the source of the Constitution, rather than the Constitution being the source of individual rights.**

Option d is incorrect. **Rule of Law is not just about obedience, it is about ensuring just, equitable laws. A law that contradicts basic societal ethos-especially constitutional values like equality, dignity, liberty-cannot claim legitimacy merely because it is "law."

statementLines: Which of the following actions is/are completely prohibited by the Constitution of India?
I. Conferment of academic titles to individuals by the State.
II. Practice of untouchability.
III. Imposition of compulsory service for public purposes by the State.
IV. Employment of children under 14 years of age in any kind of work.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: Which of the following actions is/are completely prohibited by the Constitution of India? I. Conferment of academic titles to individuals by the State. II. Practice of untouchability. III. Imposition of compulsory service for public purposes by the State. IV. Employment of children under 14 years of age in any kind of work. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. ** Option I is incorrect. Article 18 **of the Constitution of India abolishes titles. It says that "**No title, not being a military **or academic distinction, shall be conferred by the State**." Thus, it prohibits the State from granting titles**except for **military or academic distinctions**. The Supreme Court has clarified that national awards such as the Bharat Ratna, Padma Vibhushan, Padma Bhushan and Padma Shri**do not amount to titles**within the meaning of Article 18, and hence they are**not **completely prohibited.** Option II is correct.**Article 17 abolishes "Untouchability" **and** forbids its practice in any form**. It says that the enforcement of any disability arising out of "Untouchability" shall be an offence punishable in accordance with law. Article 17 of the Constitution explicitly abolishes untouchability and prohibits its practice in any form. This right is available against private individuals.** Option III is incorrect. **Article 23 provides for prohibition of traffic in human beings and forced labour. It says that Traffic in human beings and begar and other similar forms of forced labour are prohibited. However,**it allows the State from imposing compulsory service for public purposes.**Article 23(2) provides for an exception to Article 23 and permits the State to impose compulsory service for public purposes, such as a military service or social service, But, in imposing such service the State shall not make any discrimination on grounds only of religion, race, caste, or class or any of them.** Option IV is incorrect.**Article 24 reads Prohibition of employment of children in factories, etc. It prohibits the employment of children below the age of 14 years**in any factory, mine, or engaged in any other **hazardous employment. **This provision aims to protect children from exploitation and ensures their safety and well-being.**Thus, it would be incorrect to say the Constitution of India prohibits employment of children under 14 years of age **in any kind of work. Additionally According to**Section 4 **of**The Child Labour (Prohibition and Regulation) Amendment Act, 2016**, no child (one who has not completed his fourteenth year) shall be employed or permitted to work in any occupation or process.**However, the Act states that this provision shall not apply if the child: **(a)**helps his family or family enterprise**, which is other than any hazardous occupation or process set forth in the Schedule, and only after school hours or during vacations; (b) works as an**artist in the audio-visual entertainment industry, including advertisements, films, television **serials, or any such other entertainment or sports activities**except the circus, subject to such conditions and safety measures as may be prescribed:**Provided that no such work under this clause shall affect the school education of the child. **
